items.q: Which of the following is a fundamental particle: proton, electron, or neutron?
items.correct: Only the electron — protons and neutrons are made of quarks.
items.misconception: All three (proton, electron, neutron) are fundamental particles that cannot be broken down.
items.random_wrong: Only the proton is fundamental; electrons and neutrons are composite.
items.why: The atomic model is introduced with proton/neutron/electron as building blocks, so students don't update when quarks appear in later courses.

items.q: What is the difference between heat and temperature?
items.correct: Heat is the energy transferred due to temperature difference; temperature measures average kinetic energy of particles.
items.misconception: Heat and temperature are the same thing — both measure how hot something is.
items.random_wrong: Heat is measured in degrees Celsius and temperature in Joules.
items.why: Everyday language uses 'heat' and 'temperature' interchangeably ('turn up the heat'), so students don't distinguish them physically.

items.q: Why do more massive stars have shorter main-sequence lifetimes than smaller ones?
items.correct: They burn through their hydrogen fuel much faster because higher core temperature drives nuclear fusion at a vastly greater rate.
items.misconception: All stars live the same time on the main sequence regardless of mass.
items.random_wrong: More massive stars live longer because they have more fuel.
items.why: Students assume more fuel always means longer life, missing that mass increases consumption rate even more sharply than fuel supply.

items.q: A proton consists of which quarks?
items.why: Correct answer: uud. “udd” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In β⁻ decay, the exchange particle is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: W⁻ boson. “Photon” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
